title: The worst complication I’ve ever seen | Scalp Necrosis
filename: The worst complication I’ve ever seen ｜ Scalp Necrosis.en.vtt
excerpt: Recently i was at a complications conference and i met an injector from somewhere in europe who showed me one of the worst complications i have ever seen this took me back to the moment i first saw this procedure it was about two years ago and many of you seen this posted on prof…

segments.text: recently i was at a complications conference and i met an injector from somewhere in europe who showed me one of the worst complications i have ever seen this took me back to the moment i first saw this procedure it was about two years ago and many of you seen this posted on professor kotifano's page now it's important to say that in this post we're simply talking about the effectiveness of the procedure there is no discussion on safety because that was not the intent of the publication but the first thought i had when i saw it when i saw the entry point was this is exactly in the place you would put a cannula if you were trying to cannulate the superficial temple artery i did note this on the post itself but i wish i'd made more of it at the time because once i saw this complication i realized how important it is that we alert other injectors around the world that this is not just a simple inject here get a result and move

forward i mean it never really is in aesthetics anyway but i think this is a particularly dangerous way to inject i'm also not sure that the result that we're getting from this actually warrants the risk but that is for each clinician and patient to decide on their own so if you haven't seen this technique essentially it is called a temporal lifting technique and it is done using a cannula the entry point of the cannula as demonstrated in the paper that we saw posted is just pre-auricularly going up towards the scalp the problem with this entry point is that it is parallel with the large superficial temple artery which makes it extremely risky what is important is to discuss is there a safer way of getting the same result if you think that result is worth pursuing now let's first have a look back at this case and some of the psychology around a clinician who might see this particular post because i think what happens in the clinician's mind when you see a post like this from a really reputable fantastic source world-class source is it makes you think

this is probably something i should incorporate into my practice if i want to keep up with current trends and that may be true with many of these cases but i really want people to think beyond that into also what the potential risks are because new ideas are often not really well tested and you'll see this as you develop in your career many times that some new idea comes up that seems amazing and everyone tries to do it and then you find out later on it just doesn't work or it's very risky i've seen this with so many things now it's really worth waiting and thinking through these things before you take something and implement it into your practice and see what happens i think there's a large part of the fact that it's a published technique that makes people much more confident to try it than i think they actually should be remember this was not an assessment of risk it was only an assessment of effectiveness don't forget there's still a chance to win a copy of my upcoming new book and online course it's everything you need to know about lips if you like the way i described things in anatomical terms injection technique

and risk this book is going to cover that in unheard of detail so all you need to do is click on the link in the description so the next thing to consider is it possible to do this technique safely well we've got to think probabilistically about risk of course you can do that procedure and not cause a vascular occlusion in fact tomorrow i could in my clinic open up and probably do 10 of them very carefully and not cause this problem the problem is vascular occlusion is a rare event no matter how bad of an injector you are it's actually quite hard to get a blood vessel full of filler even if you're not trying not to do that but if you take a particular procedure and let a hundred thousand people do it they're all going to have different risk profiles and different severity of risk as well so severity and frequency are often confused what i really don't like about this procedure it is a high volume injection in a small area in a place where it's very hard to check capillary refill because it's in the scalp so diagnosis will be delayed and we're

also using a long cannula to get to the place where we want to go so aspiration will be less effective the mobility of the cannula will be less naturally even if you're not in a vessel and you're going to have to push relatively hard to get the cannula into place in various different patients because every patient is different and all of these are lining up for the worst possible outcome to be more frequent and that is a large bolus of filler into the superficial temple arteries blocking blood supply to the scalp and permanently scarring someone because the injury that i saw was really rather shocking it was a deep necrotic wound that completely destroyed the ability to grow hair and there was no opportunity for hair replacement surgery to benefit the patient either so this is a woman who will be scarred for life in a way that is really quite damaging for someone to have a complete loss of maybe a third of her scalp without any hair growing on it now i'm not saying there's no place for this procedure but i definitely think we should be weighing up this downside as a huge reason to

consider not doing it or being very selective with who you do it for and not simply recommending this to everyone who wants a little bit of one to two millimeters lift so the take home for me on this is just because something can be done doesn't mean that you should try it just because it has been validated in terms of results does not mean it's been validated in terms of risk and we need to be thinking through before we get the evidence what the likelihood of injury is and just because it's effective doesn't mean it's a good idea on every patient or in fact on any patient just because it's done a certain way doesn't mean there isn't a safer way to do it and i would say anything that's not parallel with the arteries is likely to be safer than this particular entry point and please don't rush into doing something because it looks superficially simple because i think that post was so well constructed that it actually does look easy to do it actually isn't there's a lot more nuance to what we do than simply the photo that makes it look simple so be careful out there take things slowly think through everything and get expert

training before you try a new procedure let me know what you thought when you first saw this procedure were you getting excited to try it or were you thinking wow that looks dangerous let me know in the comments down below [Music] you
